coredata.dc:title: Highly-efficient organic light-emitting devices based on poly(N,N′-bis-4-butylphenyl-N,N′-bisphenyl)benzidine:octadecylamine-graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Dae Hun
Kim, Tae Whan
coredata.dc:description: Organic light-emitting devices (OLEDs) with a poly(N,N′-bis-4-butylphenyl-N,N′-bisphenyl)benzidine (poly-TPD):octadecylamine (ODA)-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hole transport layer (HTL) were fabricated to enhance their efficiency. Photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation spectra showed that the optical energy bandgap of the ODA-GQDs was 2.7 eV, and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy spectra demonstrated that the edge of the highest occupied molecular orbital of the ODA-GQDs was 5.3 eV below the Fermi level. While the operating voltage of the OLEDs with a PVK:ODA-GQD (9.77 V, 0.25 wt%) HTL at 10 mA/cm2 was 0.8 V lower than that of the OLEDs with a poly-TPD HTL (10.57 V), their current efficiency was larger by more than 20% than that of the OLEDs with poly-TPD (32.88 cd/A) due to an increase in the number of holes injected from the ITO to the HTL. The number of injected holes was increased due to a reduction of the energy barrier and an increase in the conductivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic light-emitting device
Graphene quantum dot
Current efficiency
Nanocomposites
Hole transport layer

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology ( 2016R1A2A1A05005502 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the formation process for the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD film.
(a) Low-resolution and (b) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of the ODA-GQDs.
(a) C1s and (b) O1s peaks in the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra for the rGO and ODA-GQDs.
(a) Photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation spectra, and (b) ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy spectrum for the ODA-GQDs.
Atomic force microscopy images of the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films with various mass ratios of poly-TPD to ODA-GQDs.
Current density – voltages plots for hole only devices with poly-TPD:ODA-GQD hole transport layers.
Transmittances of the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films with various mass ratios of poly-TPD to ODA-GQDs.
(a) Schematic energy diagram, and (b) current density – voltage, (c) luminance – voltage, and (d) current efficiency – luminance plots for OLEDs with poly-TPD:ODA-GQD hole transport layers.

head.ce:title: Highly-efficient organic light-emitting devices based on poly(N,N′-bis-4-butylphenyl-N,N′-bisphenyl)benzidine:octadecylamine-graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronics and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronics and Computer Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organic light-emitting devices (OLEDs) with a poly(N,N′-bis-4-butylphenyl-N,N′-bisphenyl)benzidine (poly-TPD):octadecylamine (ODA)-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hole transport layer (HTL) were fabricated to enhance their efficiency. Photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation spectra showed that the optical energy bandgap of the ODA-GQDs was 2.7 eV, and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy spectra demonstrated that the edge of the highest occupied molecular orbital of the ODA-GQDs was 5.3 eV below the Fermi level. While the operating voltage of the OLEDs with a PVK:ODA-GQD (9.77 V, 0.25 wt%) HTL at 10 mA/cm2 was 0.8 V lower than that of the OLEDs with a poly-TPD HTL (10.57 V), their current efficiency was larger by more than 20% than that of the OLEDs with poly-TPD (32.88 cd/A) due to an increase in the number of holes injected from the ITO to the HTL. The number of injected holes was increased due to a reduction of the energy barrier and an increase in the conductivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic light-emitting devices (OLEDs) have been receiving considerable attention for potential applications in flexible displays and solid-state lighting [1–4]. The prospect for promising applications of OLEDs has led to substantial research and development efforts to decrease their operating voltage and to enhance their efficiency [5,6]. The optimization of the charges injected from the electrode into the emitting layer is designed to achieve a low operating voltage and a high efficiency due to the low intrinsic carrier concentration of the organic materials [7]. However, the large energy barrier between the work function of the indium-tin oxide (ITO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the hole transporting layer (HTL) restricts the efficient flow of the injected holes from the ITO to the HTL [8,9]. A variety of routes have been proposed to facilitate hole injection utilizing the advantages of special materials such as tungsten oxide, molybdenum trioxide, vanadium oxide, and nickel oxide [10–14]. However, almost all of the oxides are relatively toxic to both humans and the environment. In particular, when nanoparticles of metal oxides are synthesized, the toxicity of the metal oxides may pose some serious problems [15].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene fragments with sizes less than 20 nm and with special quantum confinement effects and edge effects [16]. Recently, GQDs have been used extensively as carrier acceptors due to the ultrafast injection of carrier through the GQDs from the carrier extraction layer to the active layer of the photovoltaic (PV) cells [17]. When the GQDs are inserted into the hole transport layer of the PV cells, the carrier conductance of the PEDOT:PSS layer is increased, and its light scattering length is extended [18]. Furthermore, GQDs have excellent physical properties: non-zero band-gap, strong excitation luminesce due to the quantum confinement and the edge effects, simple band-gap tuning, low toxicity, high chemical stability, and excellent carrier transport mobility [19–23]. However, the existence of numerous oxygenous functional groups and defects in the GQDs decreases their electron mobility, and because of the hydrophilic behavior of the GQDs, they are not uniformly dispersed in the hydrophobic HTL solution, resulting in limited HTL material selection [24].

In this paper, we demonstrate enhancement of the current efficiency for OLEDs using a poly(N,N′-bis-4-butylphenyl-N,N′-bisphenyl)benzidine (poly-TPD):octadecylamine (ODA)-GQD HTL formed by using a simple solution method. The ODA-GQDs have high conductivity and good dispersal properties with a poly-TPD solution. Also, because the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) level of the ODA-GQDs is located between the work function of the ITO and the HOMO level of the poly-TPD, the poly-TPD:ODA-GQDs were used as the HTL of the OLEDs. The efficiency enhancement of the OLEDs with ODA-GQDs is attributed to the improved efficiency for injecting holes from the anode to the active layer, resulting in a higher current efficiency [25]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed to investigate the microstructural properties of the ODA-GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to investigate the nucleophilic substitution in the ODA-GQDs. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out to investigate the optical properties of the ODA-GQDs. Current density - voltage - luminance (J-V-L) measurements were carried out to investigate the electrical and the optical properties of the OLEDs fabricated utilizing ODA-GQDs.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the method used in this research to fabricate the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films. The details of the process for fabricating the ODA-GQDs are given elsewhere [26]. A mixed powder of ODA and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was dissolved in methanol at a weight ratio of 20:1. After the mixed solution had been stirred for 24 h by using a spin-bar, an ODA-rGO flakes were formed due to nucleophilic substitution. The ODA-rGO flakes were washed with methanol to remove the residue. The ODA-rGO flake powder was dried at 100 °C. The ODA-rGO powder was dissolved in a mixed solution of chlorobenzene and methanol with a volume ratio of 50:1. Next, the ODA-rGO solution was mixed for 3 h by using an ultrasonic method, resulting in the formation of the ODA-GQDs. After this solution had been centrifuged, the upper portion of the liquid in the centrifuge tube was extracted. The poly-TPD was dissolved in the ODA-GQDs solution, and the mixed solution was stirred for 10 h by using a spin-bar. The poly-TPD:ODA-GQD solution was deposited onto an ITO-coated glass substrate, resulting in the formation of a poly-TPD:ODA-GQD film.

The sheet resistivity and the thickness of the ITO thin films coated on the glass substrates were 15 Ω/square and 150 nm, respectively. The ITO-coated glass substrates were cleaned in acetone and methanol at 25 °C for 20 min each by using an ultrasonic cleaner and were thoroughly rinsed in de-ionized water. After the chemically-cleaned ITO-coated glass substrates had been dried by using N2 gas with a purity of 99.9999%, the surfaces of the ITO-coated glass substrates were treated with an ultraviolet-ozone gas for 20 min at room temperature. The poly-TPD:ODA-GQD solution was deposited onto the ITO-coated glass substrate by spin-coating at 5000 rpm for 61 s and was then annealed at 145 °C for 15 min in a glove box. After the samples had been transferred into an evaporation chamber, the organic layers and the electrodes were deposited at a substrate temperature of 25 °C and a system pressure of 1.7 × 10−4 Pa. The deposition rates of the organic layers and the metal layer were approximately 1.0 and 2.0 Å/s, respectively.

The TEM images were measured by using a JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The XPS and the UPS spectra were measured by using a theta probe base system purchased from the Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. The UPS spectra were obtained at a system pressure of 6.7 × 10−10 Pa with a HeI 21.2-eV source. The Raman measurements were obtained by using an IS50 model spectrometer. The PL measurements were performed by using an X-ray fluorescence spectrometer (TCSPC-FL920) equipped with a pulsed laser operating at 365 nm. The current-voltage characteristics were measured on a programmable electrometer with built-in current and voltage measurement units (M6100, McScience). The brightness intensities were measured by using a brightness meter, and the electroluminescence (EL) spectra were measured by using a luminescence spectrometer (CS-1000, Minolta). All measurements of the electrical characteristics for the OLEDs were carried out at 25 °C under atmospheric conditions.

Fig. 2 shows (a) low-resolution and (b) high-resolution TEM images of the ODA-GQDs. The diameter of the ODA-GQDs was approximately 40 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). The TEM image demonstrated that GQDs had been embedded in the ODA nanoparticles, resulting in the formation of the ODA-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The ODA-GQDs were formed by the aggregation of ODA chains onto the GQDs. The average size of the GQDs was about 4.2 nm while the average lattice fringe for the GQDs in an ODA nanoparticle was approximately 0.22 nm.

Fig. 3 shows the C1s and the O1s peaks in the XPS spectra for the rGO and the ODA-GQDs. The dominant peaks at 284.5, 286.5, 287.1, and 288.1 eV in the XPS spectrum correspond to the CC bond and to the presence of the oxygen functional groups of carbon atoms for the COH bond, COC bond, and CO bond for the rGO, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3(a) [27]. The peak intensities related to the oxygen functional groups of carbon atoms in the XPS spectra of the ODA-GQDs were decreased in comparison with those corresponding to the rGO. The decreases in the peak intensities for the oxygen functional groups for the carbon atoms originated from the nucleophilic substitution between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the rGO [28]. The dominant peaks at 531.5 and 533 eV for the XPS spectrum correspond to the CO and the CO bonds of rGO, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3(b). However, while the peak due to the CO bonds for the ODA-GQD was not observed, the peak due to the CO bonds was observed due to the substitution between the epoxy functional groups and the amine functional groups. The peak at 531.5 eV was not observed as a result of the NH group of ODA sticking to the surface of a GQD instead of the OH group. Furthermore, the intensity of the peak at 533 eV was increased due to formation of CONH groups [29].

Fig. 4 shows the PL, PL excitation, and UPS spectra for the ODA-GQDs. The peaks in the PL and the PL excitation spectra intersected 463 nm, as shown in Fig. 4(a). The intersection peak of the ODA-GQDs at 463 nm corresponds to their optical energy bandgap. The optical energy bandgap of the ODA-GQDs was 2.7 eV. Fig. 4(b) shows the UPS spectra of ODA-GQDs. The edge of the HOMO of the ODA-GQDs is seen to be 5.3 eV below the Fermi level, and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of the GQDs is seen to be 2.6 eV. While the numerous oxygenous functional groups existing in the GQDs limit the magnitude of the flow of electrons, the existence of the ODA-GQDs increases the mobility of the electrons due to the substitution between the epoxy functional groups and the amine functional groups [24].

Fig. 5 shows AFM images of the poly-TPD and the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films. The root-mean-square roughness values of (a) the poly-TPD film and the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films ((b) 0.1, (c) 0.25, (d) 0.5, and (e) 1 wt%) are 0.6, 0.7, 1.0, 2.4, and 13.0 nm, respectively. The morphologies of the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films (0.1 and 0.25 wt%) are as smooth as that of the poly-TPD film, being below 1 nm, which is due to the uniform dispersion of the ODA-GQDs embedded in the poly-TPD. However, when the ratio of the ODA-GQDs is increased by more than 0.5%, the root-mean-square roughness values of the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films increases due to an agglomeration of the ODA-GQDs.

Fig. 6 shows the J-V characteristics of the hole-only devices with poly-TPD and poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1%). The hole-only devices are used to compare the numbers of injected holes at the interface between the ITO elecrode and the HTLs. The current density of the hole-only device is mainly determined by the injected holes because electrons injected from the Al elecrode are blocked by the TAPC layer. The operation voltages of the hole-only devices with poly-TPD and poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1%) are 3.9, 3.4, 3.1, 4.2, and 4.7 V, respectively. The operating voltage of the hole-only device with a PVK:ODA-GQD (0.25%) HTL is seen to be 0.8 V lower than that of the device with a poly-TPD HTL. As the ratio of ODA-GQDs is increased to 0.25%, the operating voltage of hole-only device decreases due to the decreased energy barrier and high conductivity. On the other hand, when the ratio of ODA-GQDs is increased by more than 0.5%, the hole flow is limited by the agglomeration of ODA-GQDs, resulting in an increased operating voltage [30].

Fig. 7 shows the transmittances of the poly-TPD:ODA-GQDs films with various mass ratios of poly-TPD to ODA-GQDs. The transmittances of the poly-TPD and the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films are above 95% in the wavelength range between 450 and 800 nm. The transmittances of the poly-TPD and the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, or 1 wt%) are 96.9, 97.4, 97.5, 97.5, and 97.5%, respectively. While the transmittance of the poly-TPD film at 520 nm is 96.9%, that of the poly-TPD:ODA-GQDs at 520 nm is 97.5%. The trasmittances of the poly-TPD:ODA-GQD films are almost the same, regardless of the variation in the mass ratio of poly-TPD to ODA-GQDs, indicating that the ODA-GQDs do not affect the optical properties of the devices.

Fig. 8 (a) shows a schematic energy diagram of the OLEDs with a poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTL to clarify the enhanced efficiency mechanisms. The structure of the OLEDs consists of an indium-tin-oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrate/poly-TPD:ODA-GQDs (40 nm)/1,1-Bis[(di-4-tolylamino)phenyl]cyclohexane (TAPC) (40 nm)/4,4′-Bis(N-carbazolyl)-1,1′-biphenyl (CBP): tris[2-phenylpyridinato-C2,N]iridium(III) (Ir(ppy)3) (30 nm)/2,2′,2"-(1,3,5-Benzinetriyl)-tris(1-phenyl-1-H-benzimidazole) (TPBi) (30 nm)/Li (1 nm)/Al electrode (100 nm). While the electrons emitted from the Li/Al are transferred from the TPBi to the active layers, the holes in the ITO flow from the poly-TPD:ODQ-GQD layer to the TAPC/active layer due to the existence of an electric field [31]. The energy barrier of 0.8 eV between the work function of the ITO elecrode and the HOMO of the poly-TPD interrupts the efficient flow of holes. A smaller number of flowing holes results in a decrease in the current efficiency for the OLEDs due to a reduction in the number of electron-hole pairs in the active layer. However, because the energy barrier of 0.6 eV between the work function of the ITO and the HOMO of the ODA-GQDs is lower than that of 0.8 eV between the ITO and the poly-TPDs, the number of injected holes is increased, resulting in an enhancement of the current efficiency for the OLEDs with a poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTL. The operating voltages of the OLEDs with poly-TPD and poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, or 1 wt%) at 10 mA/cm2 were 10.57, 10.27, 9.77, 10.90, and 11.82 V, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8(b). The operating voltage of the OLEDs with a PVK:ODA-GQD (0.25 wt%) HTL was lower than that of the OLEDs with a poly-TPD HTL due to an enhancement in the number of injected holes resulting from a decrease in the energy barrier. The operating voltages of the OLEDs with poly-TPD and poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, or 1 wt%) at 100 cd/m2 were 6.68, 6.17, 5.90, 6.73, and 8.13 V, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8(c). The maximum values of the current efficiencies for the OLEDs with poly-TPD and poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, or 1 wt%) at 100 cd/m2 were 32.88, 31.93, 39.77, 38.11 and 8.05 cd/A, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8(d). The current efficiency of the OLED with a poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTL (0.25 wt%) was 6.89 cd/A larger than that of the OLED with a poly-TPD HTL due to an increase in the number of holes injected from the ITO into the poly-TPD HTL, which resulted from the decreased energy barrier and the high conductivity of the ODA-GQDs [32]. The current efficiency of the OLED with a poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTL (1 wt%) was dramatically decreased in comparison with that of the OLED with a poly-TPD HTL because the flow of holes was interrupted due to the agglomeration of ODA-GQDs in the poly-TPD layer. The efficiency enhancement of the OLEDs with poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs originates from an increase in the number of holes directly injected from the ITO to the ODA-GQDs.

OLEDs were fabricated with poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs to enhance their efficiencies. XPS spectra showed that the oxygen content of the ODA-GQDs was smaller than that of the typical GQDs due to nucleophilic substitution, indicative of possible applications of ODA-GQDs in carrier transport layers. The PL and the PL excitation spectra showed that the optical energy bandgap of the ODA-GQDs was 2.7 eV, and the UPS spectra demonstrated that the edge of the HOMO of the ODA-GQDs was 5.3 eV below the Fermi level. The current efficiency of the OLED with a poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTL (39.77 cd/A, 0.25 wt%) was 6.89 cd/A larger than that of the OLED with a poly-TPD HTL (32.88 cd/A). The efficiency enhancements of the OLEDs with poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs were attributed to an increase in the number of holes injected from the ITO to the HTL, which resulted from a decrease in the energy barrier and the high conductivity of ODA-GQDs. These results indicate that OLEDs with a high current efficiency based on poly-TPD:ODA-GQD HTLs hold promise for potential applications in flexible display systems based on GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2016R1A2A1A05005502).
